text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Maria Elena Reyes
Age: 45 years
Diagnosis: Stage IIB (cT2N1M0 → ypT1cN1M0) triple-negative breast cancer, left breast, grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative, PD-L1 CPS 18

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Routine oncology follow-up during adjuvant systemic therapy. Patient presents for Cycle 5 of adjuvant capecitabine and interval assessment. She has also been noted to have a mildly elevated TSH on recent labs, prompting discussion today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Reyes is a 45-year-old premenopausal woman with a diagnosis of Stage IIB TNBC of the left breast established approximately 14 months ago. She is well known to our service. To briefly summarize her treatment course: she completed neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks (6 doses during neoadjuvant phase) in combination with dose-dense AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide, 4 cycles q2 weeks), followed by weekly paclitaxel (12 doses; reduced to 64 mg/m2 x 4 doses for Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy) over approximately 20 total weeks of neoadjuvant therapy. She then underwent left breast-conserving surgery (partial mastectomy with wire localization) and completion axillary lymph node dissection. Surgical pathology confirmed residual disease: ypT1cN1, with a 1.2 cm focus of residual invasive ductal carcinoma and 3 of 16 positive lymph nodes (RCB score 2.8, RCB-II, non-pCR). She subsequently completed adjuvant locoregional radiation to the left breast, supraclavicular fossa, axilla, and internal mammary chain (whole breast 50 Gy + 10 Gy boost; regional nodal irradiation included), finishing approximately 6 weeks post-surgery. She developed Grade 2 radiation dermatitis during RT (managed with silver sulfadiazine and a brief treatment break) and Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis approximately 6 weeks after RT completion (managed with low-dose prednisone with taper, now resolved).

Following radiation, she transitioned to adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 PO BID x 14 days/21-day cycle (per CREATE-X), along with continuation of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks for approximately 1 year total (neoadjuvant + adjuvant). DPYD genotyping was performed prior to capecitabine initiation and was wildtype, confirming no dose adjustment needed on that basis. During Cycle 3 of capecitabine, she developed Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) with bilateral palmar/plantar erythema, swelling, and tenderness, as well as Grade 2 diarrhea (4–6 loose stools per day above baseline). Capecitabine was dose-reduced by 25% to 750 mg/m2 BID for Cycle 3 and onward. Diarrhea was managed with loperamide and resolved within 5 days. Hand-foot syndrome subsequently improved.

She is now presenting for Cycle 5, Day 1 of capecitabine at the reduced dose of 750 mg/m2 BID. She has received 14 of 17 planned total pembrolizumab doses and is on track to complete her full year of pembro. She is today scheduled to receive pembrolizumab dose 14 as well, as cycles align today.

Of note, routine labs drawn last week revealed a TSH of 7.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated) with a free T4 in the low-normal range. This is a new finding compared to prior TSH values, which have been within normal limits throughout therapy. Given her prolonged pembrolizumab exposure, this is most consistent with immune-mediated hypothyroidism, a recognized and expected immune-related adverse event (irAE) of checkpoint inhibitor therapy. She denies frank hypothyroid symptoms — no significant fatigue beyond her treatment baseline, no cold intolerance, no constipation, no weight gain, no myalgias or proximal muscle weakness, no cognitive changes, and no edema. However, she does report feeling somewhat more tired than her prior baseline over the past few weeks, which she had attributed to ongoing capecitabine. It is possible the subclinical hypothyroidism is contributing.

Surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed approximately one month ago showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Post-radiation changes in the left lung base (minor resolving ground-glass opacity, consistent with prior radiation pneumonitis) were noted and deemed stable/improving. No hepatic lesions, no new adenopathy, no osseous abnormalities.

She reports that hand-foot syndrome has settled to what she describes as Grade 1 — mild redness and tenderness of her palms and soles that is present but manageable and does not interfere with her ability to work or perform daily activities. She has been compliant with urea-based emollient cream (Eucerin) twice daily and is avoiding heat/friction per instructions. She denies any new gastrointestinal complaints (no diarrhea, no mucositis, no nausea). Peripheral neuropathy from paclitaxel persists as a very mild tingling in the fingertips, bothering her only occasionally; she grades it as Grade 1 and stable.

She has returned to part-time teaching. Her hair has begun to regrow. She is emotionally engaged with her support group and continues individual therapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, improved from prior. No fevers, no chills, no unintentional weight loss.
Skin: Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome as described. No new rashes. No bullous lesions. Irradiated left breast skin — mildly hyperpigmented, no moist desquamation, healed from radiation dermatitis.
Head/Eyes/ENT: No visual changes, no diplopia, no dry eyes. No oral ulcers or mucositis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: Mild intermittent dry cough, much improved from prior. No shortness of breath at rest or with exertion. No hemoptysis. (History of Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis, now resolved clinically.)
Gastrointestinal: Good oral intake. No diarrhea currently. No abdominal pain. No nausea.
Musculoskeletal: No significant joint pain or stiffness. (Prior immune-related arthralgia during neoadjuvant therapy resolved after prednisone taper; no recurrence.)
Neurologic: Mild residual fingertip tingling (Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy, stable). No motor weakness. No headaches, no cognitive difficulties.
Endocrine: Mild fatigue as noted; denies other overt hypothyroid symptoms as detailed above. Menstrual cycles resumed post-chemotherapy (chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea resolved by approximately 9 months post-initiation of chemotherapy). Currently having regular cycles.
Psychiatric: Mild cancer-related anxiety, managed with individual therapy. No active depression. Sleeping adequately.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 14, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 70.2 kg (BMI 26.6; essentially unchanged from baseline).

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Good affect. Wearing a wig (noted with regrowth visible at hairline).

Skin: Mild palmar erythema bilaterally, more pronounced on the thenar and hypothenar eminences; no blistering, no skin breakdown, no fissuring. Plantar surface of bilateral feet: mild diffuse erythema with no ulceration. Left breast: post-radiation hyperpigmentation of the lateral and inferior breast skin; no erythema, edema, or skin breakdown. Left axillary scar well-healed. Left breast conserving surgery scar healing well.

Head and Neck: No thyromegaly or thyroid nodularity palpable. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, no wheezes, no pleural rub. Good air entry throughout.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Breast/Chest Wall: Left breast: post-lumpectomy deformity present in the upper outer quadrant; no palpable mass or nodularity in the surgical bed. No skin dimpling or nipple retraction. Right breast: no palpable mass, no axillary adenopathy.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable bilateral axillary adenopathy. No supraclavicular or infraclavicular adenopathy. No cervical adenopathy.

Extremities: No clubbing. No lower extremity edema. Bilateral mild palmar/plantar erythema as noted under skin exam.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Sensation to light touch mildly diminished in fingertips bilaterally (consistent with residual Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy). Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Reflexes symmetrical. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC (within past 7 days):
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (reference 4.5–11.0); differential: neutrophils 65%, lymphocytes 24%, monocytes 8%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 1%
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mildly low, Grade 1 anemia; stable, likely residual from prior chemotherapy and chronic mild anemia of treatment)
- Platelets: 198 x10^9/L (normal)
- ANC: 3.77 x10^9/L (normal, no neutropenia)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Creatinine: 0.72 mg/dL (normal); eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL; AST: 28 U/L; ALT: 22 U/L; alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L (all within normal limits; no hepatotoxicity)
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 7.8 mIU/L (HIGH; normal 0.4–4.0 mIU/L)
- Free T4: 0.78 ng/dL (low-normal; reference 0.8–1.8 ng/dL)
- Free T3: not ordered at this time
- Thyroid antibodies (anti-TPO, anti-thyroglobulin): sent, pending

Other Endocrine:
- Cortisol (AM): 16.2 mcg/dL (normal; no adrenal insufficiency)
- Fasting glucose: 94 mg/dL (normal)
- HbA1c: 5.4% (normal; no drug-induced diabetes)

Tumor Markers (obtained per institutional surveillance protocol):
- CA 27-29: 18 U/mL (normal)
- CEA: 1.8 ng/mL (normal)

---

IMAGING REVIEWED

Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 4 weeks ago): No evidence of metastatic disease. The left lung base shows minor residual ground-glass opacity, which appears smaller compared to the prior study and is consistent with resolving post-radiation change from her Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis — this is not a concern. The left axillary surgical bed shows expected post-surgical scarring and fibrosis, no new nodes. The liver is clean. No concerning bony lesions. Overall a reassuring study.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IIB TNBC, left breast (ypT1cN1M0, RCB-II, non-pCR), currently on adjuvant therapy — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE on most recent imaging.

Patient is approximately 7 months out from surgery and doing well. She has tolerated the adjuvant regimen with expected toxicities, all manageable. Surveillance CT is clean. Plan to continue with current adjuvant treatment program.

2. Adjuvant capecitabine — Cycle 5, Day 1 today.

Will proceed with capecitabine 750 mg/m2 PO BID x 14 days (reduced from the original 1000 mg/m2 due to prior Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome, per our dose modification at Cycle 3). Hand-foot syndrome is currently at Grade 1 and manageable at this reduced dose. She is compliant with emollient therapy. No further dose reduction needed at this time. Prescriptions provided for Cycle 5 (Days 1–14). She was reminded to maintain emollient use, avoid hot water immersion of hands/feet, and wear cushioned footwear. Diarrhea has resolved — she has a supply of loperamide for PRN use. She will complete 3 more cycles after today (Cycles 5, 6, 7, 8) to finish the full CREATE-X 8-cycle course.

3. Pembrolizumab — Dose 14 of 17 administered today.

Patient has received 14 of a planned 17 total pembrolizumab doses (6 during neoadjuvant therapy + 8 adjuvant doses to date), consistent with KEYNOTE-522 protocol targeting approximately 1 year of total pembrolizumab. Pembro 200 mg IV administered in infusion center today without incident. Immune review performed: no colitis (no diarrhea), no active pneumonitis (lungs clear, prior radiation pneumonitis resolved), no hepatitis (LFTs normal), no adrenal insufficiency (morning cortisol normal), no new arthralgias (prior irAE resolved), no skin toxicity beyond expected. The new finding of hypothyroidism (see below) is the only active irAE at present and is being managed appropriately. Checkpoint inhibitor toxicity checklist reviewed and documented. Plan: continue pembro on schedule for 3 additional doses.

4. New: Immune-mediated hypothyroidism, pembrolizumab-related — Grade 1–2.

New elevation in TSH (7.8 mIU/L) with low-normal free T4 in the setting of ongoing pembrolizumab. This is consistent with checkpoint inhibitor-induced primary hypothyroidism, an expected and manageable irAE occurring in approximately 8–15% of patients on pembrolizumab. Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies are pending. Patient is largely asymptomatic though reports mild fatigue, which may be partially attributable to this. Per KEYNOTE-522 protocol and standard irAE management guidelines, hypothyroidism of this degree (TSH elevated but below 10, patient asymptomatic) does not require pembrolizumab interruption. Initiating levothyroxine 50 mcg PO once daily (to be taken on an empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before breakfast). Will recheck TSH and free T4 in 6 weeks. Endocrinology consultation placed for co-management. She was counseled that this is likely a permanent effect and she may require thyroid hormone replacement indefinitely. Pembrolizumab continued without dose modification.

5. Residual Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy (paclitaxel-related) — stable.

Very mild fingertip tingling. Not worsening, not interfering with function. No active pharmacologic treatment. Will monitor at each visit. Given that she is off paclitaxel, further improvement over time is anticipated but not guaranteed.

6. Grade 1 anemia — stable.

Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, mild, asymptomatic, no reticulocytopenia. Likely residual from prior intensive chemotherapy. Iron studies obtained today (pending). Will review at next visit. No transfusion indication.

7. Psychosocial and quality of life.

She is actively engaged in a peer support group for younger women with breast cancer and continues individual therapy for cancer-related anxiety and body image concerns. She has returned to part-time teaching and reports meaningful improvement in quality of life compared to the peak of her treatment. She was encouraged to continue psychosocial supports. She was reminded that the neuropathy, fatigue, and hair changes will continue to improve. Discussed her interest in future pregnancy; deferred detailed counseling to a planned reproductive endocrinology referral, though we briefly reviewed that current NCCN guidance generally suggests a waiting period of at least 2 years post-treatment for TNBC before attempting conception, recognizing this remains individualized.

8. Next visit and follow-up plan.

- Return in approximately 3 weeks for Cycle 6 of capecitabine and pembrolizumab dose 15.
- Repeat TSH and free T4 in 6 weeks (after levothyroxine initiation).
- Review anti-thyroid antibody results at next visit.
- Review iron studies at next visit.
- Annual breast MRI and mammography scheduled per surveillance protocol.
- Continue clinical breast exam at each visit.
- Endocrinology follow-up being arranged.

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes, of which more than 50% was counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by:
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
